turns.content: I'm trying to migrate our old API schema to a new one using serde, but I don't understand how to handle fields that existed in the past but not now. My current code just skips them and I think it will break on existing clients.

You are confusing forward compatibility with backward compatibility; those are different things. For new data, you can drop fields, but for old data arriving at your server, you need to handle missing fields gracefully using #[serde(default)] on the struct field so that serde will use a default value instead of panicking when the field is absent.
Ah okay, so I should just add #[serde(default)] to my struct and it will work. But what if an old client sends data with extra fields that my new schema doesn't expect?

That is the other direction you need to worry about; your code should ignore unknown fields using #[serde(skip_deserialize)] or #[skip_serializing_if] on specific fields depending on whether they are in incoming JSON or outgoing requests. The key pattern for versioning is often an enum variant that acts as a version tag so you can deserialize the message into different structs based on which version was sent.
I see, so I should define something like Message where each variant represents a major version? And then use #[serde(tag = "version")] to tell serde which struct to fill in?

Exactly. You would define an enum with variants for v1, v2, etc., and annotate it with #[serde(tag = "version", content = "data")]. Then when a client sends v1 data, serde will deserialize the "data" field into your V1 struct, which can ignore new fields that don't exist yet.
I'm working on a tutorial about versioning, and I don't get the difference between using #[serde(default)] versus defining explicit default values in my struct.

Great question. If you use #[serde(default)], Serde will fill missing fields with a default value when deserializing, which is great for forward compatibility where newer structs have extra fields that older versions don't know about.
But what if I just put the defaults inside the struct definition? Is there any real difference or am I overthinking this?

There is a key difference. If you define explicit defaults in the struct, those values are always used regardless of whether the data was missing or present. With #[serde(default)], Serde only applies them when the field is actually absent from the input stream.
So if I send a request with all fields filled in, #[serde(default)] won't touch anything but explicit defaults will still apply?

Exactly. Think of it like this: explicit defaults are like pre-filled forms you always submit, while #[serde(default)] is like a system that only fills in blanks on the form when someone leaves them empty.
I see now. So for migration scenarios where old data might lack fields, I should prefer #[serde(default)] to avoid breaking old clients?

That's a good instinct, but you also need to consider using an enum as your version tag so the deserializer knows which schema to apply. Combining both approaches handles backward compatibility much better.
One last thing though, does #[serde(default)] work if I'm serializing instead of deserializing?

No, #[serde(default)] only applies during deserialization. If you want to ensure all fields are present when sending data out, you should use #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")] or validate in your code logic instead.
